metadata.summary: Modern Python dependency management with deterministic builds through lock files, virtual environment management, and streamlined publishing using Poetry 2.x with PEP 621 compliance.

content.title: Poetry Project Setup
content.summary: Poetry is the modern standard for Python dependency management, providing deterministic builds through lock files, virtual environment management, and streamlined publishing. As of 2025-2026, Poetry 2.x introduces PEP 621 compliance, making pyproject.toml portable across build tools. Key capabilities include dependency resolution with lock files, virtual environment management, package building and publishing, dependency groups (dev, test, docs), and PEP 621-compliant metadata.

why.p: Poetry solves the reproducibility and complexity problems of pip-based workflows. Lock files (poetry.lock) guarantee that every developer and CI environment installs identical dependency versions, eliminating "works on my machine" failures. The tool's dependency resolver handles transitive constraints that pip leaves to chance, and the group-based organization (dev, test, docs) scales better than hand-maintained requirements*.txt files that accumulate and diverge.

Poetry 2.x's adoption of PEP 621 standard metadata in [project] sections means projects are no longer locked into Poetry; they can use other tools (uv, hatch, flit) with the same configuration. This portability reduces lock-in and lets teams adopt faster resolvers for CI without rewriting project metadata.

list.item: New Python project with 3.11+ targeting long-term maintenance and reproducible builds.
Complex dependency trees where transitive conflicts matter (e.g., FastAPI + SQLAlchemy + multiple data science libraries).
Publishing to PyPI — Poetry automates build and publish workflows that manual setuptools require as custom CI steps.
Monorepo with local packages — Poetry's path dependencies with develop=true allow shared code without publishing to an index.
Team environments where lock file exactness prevents subtle dependency drift between developers.
One-shot scripts and throwaway experiments — the setup overhead (poetry new, venv, pyproject.toml) exceeds utility; use uv or plain pip instead.
CI/CD where install time is a bottleneck — Poetry's resolver is slower (sometimes 5-30 min on large scientific stacks); uv is 10-100x faster.
Memory-constrained agents or containers under 512 MB — Poetry resolver can spike to 1-2 GB on large dependency trees and cause OOM-kill.
Repos standardized on uv, pdm, or hatch — do not introduce a second tool; migrate fully or stick with the established one.
Cross-language monorepos with Bazel/Pants — those build systems already pin versions; Poetry adds redundant pinning.

section.name: poetry-2-x-key-changes
section.p: Poetry 2.0 (released January 2025) introduced significant changes from the 1.x series. The primary shift is metadata location: Poetry 1.x used [tool.poetry] for everything; Poetry 2.x adopts PEP 621's [project] section for metadata and PEP 508 syntax for dependencies.

rule.statement: Use Poetry 2.x with PEP 621 [project] section for all new projects. Migrate from Poetry 1.x using poetry-plugin-migrate if required. Do NOT hand-edit pyproject.toml dependency tables; use poetry add/remove/update commands exclusively.
rule.rationale: PEP 621 metadata is standard-compliant and portable; non-Poetry tools can read it. The migration plugin handles edge cases (dynamic versioning, plugins) that manual conversion misses. Hand-editing creates lock file desync.

example.title: Poetry 2.x with PEP 621
example.code:
[project] name = "my-project" version = "1.0.0" description = "Application description" requires-python = ">=3.11,<4.0" dependencies = [ "fastapi>=0.115.0,<1.0.0", "uvicorn[standard]>=0.32.0", ]
[tool.poetry] package-mode = true
[tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies] pytest = "^8.3" mypy = "^1.13"
[build-system] requires = ["poetry-core>=2.0.0"] build-backend = "poetry.core.masonry.api"
example.note: Metadata in [project], tools in [tool.poetry.group.*], build-system pinned to >=2.0.0.

example.title: Poetry 1.x format (deprecated)
example.code:
[tool.poetry] name = "my-project" version = "1.0.0" description = "Application description" authors = ["Your Name <you@example.com>"]
[tool.poetry.dependencies] python = "^3.11" fastapi = "^0.115"
example.note: Metadata in [tool.poetry], Poetry-specific dependency syntax. Deprecated; use PEP 621 instead.

antipattern.description: Running poetry migrate and then manually deleting the [tool.poetry] section to "clean up" the file.
antipattern.reason: Poetry 2.x transition still uses [tool.poetry] for dynamic versioning, plugins, and optional dependency groups. Deleting it breaks configuration.

section.name: core-concepts
section.p: Poetry manages three core concepts: the project metadata and dependencies (pyproject.toml), the locked snapshot of exact versions (poetry.lock), and the isolated virtual environment. Understanding their interactions prevents common pitfalls.

rule.statement: Always commit both pyproject.toml AND poetry.lock to version control. Never commit .venv/ (add to .gitignore). After any dependency change, verify poetry check passes and poetry lock --check confirms lock matches pyproject.
rule.rationale: pyproject.toml is the source; poetry.lock is the reproducible snapshot. Together they guarantee identical installs across machines. .venv/ is large and environment-specific; CI will regenerate it from lock.

example.title: Dependency groups organized
example.code:
[project.dependencies] fastapi = ">=0.115,<1.0" sqlalchemy = {version = ">=2.0", extras = ["asyncio"]}
[tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies] pytest = "^8.3" mypy = "^1.13" ruff = "^0.8"
[tool.poetry.group.docs] optional = true
[tool.poetry.group.docs.dependencies] mkdocs = "^1.6" mkdocs-material = "^9.5"
example.note: Main deps in [project.dependencies], dev/docs separated, docs marked optional so `poetry install` skips them unless --with docs.

example.title: Mixed versions with no lock file
example.code: # Manual installation without poetry.lock $ pip install fastapi $ pip install sqlalchemy # Later, colleague installs $ pip install fastapi $ pip install sqlalchemy # Different versions installed; tests fail on CI
example.note: No lock guarantees; transitive dependencies diverge silently.

section.name: dependency-groups-and-virtual-environments
section.p: Poetry's dependency groups organize dev, test, and optional dependencies cleanly. Virtual environments are isolated Python contexts that prevent global package pollution. Proper configuration of both ensures fast installs and clean CI/CD.

rule.statement: Set poetry config virtualenvs.in-project true so .venv lives in the project root. Declare dependency groups: dev (lint, type, format), test (pytest stack), docs (optional mkdocs). Do not mix concerns within a single group.
rule.rationale: .venv in-project lets agents and CI introspect Python without poetry run overhead. Group separation makes it clear what dependencies are for what phase; tests don't pull lint tools, docs don't pull test framework.

example.title: Virtual env in project, groups separated
example.code: poetry config virtualenvs.in-project true poetry install --with dev --with test --without docs # .venv/ now lives at project root # python -c works without 'poetry run' wrapper
example.note: Single config; reusable in CI via --with/--without flags.

example.title: Group confusion
example.code: [tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies] pytest = "^8.3" # Testing belongs in 'test' group mypy = "^1.13" ruff = "^0.8" mkdocs = "^1.6" # Docs tool in dev; CI pulls it unnecessarily
example.note: Everything in dev; CI installs test deps + doc tools even when only linting.

section.name: poetry-vs-uv-vs-pip
section.p: Three mainstream Python dependency managers exist. Each has strengths; the right choice depends on project type, install speed requirements, and team familiarity.

rule.statement: Choose Poetry for long-lived projects with PyPI publishing or team/monorepo use. Choose uv for new greenfield projects prioritizing CI speed or memory-constrained environments. Choose pip only for simple single-file scripts or legacy projects already standardized on it.
rule.rationale: Poetry's lock file and group management are mature and essential for teams. uv's resolver is 10-100x faster and uses less memory, making it ideal for CI and experiments. pip is the fallback for compatibility and simplicity when build complexity doesn't justify a tool.

example.title: Poetry for a publishable library
example.code: poetry new my-library cd my-library poetry add pydantic httpx poetry add -G dev pytest mypy ruff poetry add -G docs mkdocs poetry build poetry publish # Handles wheel + sdist, uploads to PyPI
example.note: Build, metadata, and publishing all integrated.

example.title: uv for CI speed
example.code: # GitHub Actions CI script - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v1 - run: uv lock - run: uv pip install -r requirements.txt # Completes in seconds, not minutes
example.note: uv resolves and installs much faster than Poetry, ideal for CI that runs frequently.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents that manage Poetry must follow strict rules to avoid lock file corruption, version conflicts, and venv desync. Never edit files by hand; always use CLI.

rule.statement: Agents MUST never Edit pyproject.toml dependency tables by hand. Call poetry add/remove/update via Bash, then verify with poetry check and poetry lock --check. If poetry install fails, run poetry lock --no-update to preserve constraints, then paste the error verbatim; do not retry silently.
rule.rationale: poetry.lock is generated; hand-edits create corruption that only poetry lock can repair. Long resolves (5-30 min) can time out and retry, causing cascading lock file rewrites. Explicit error handling prevents agent loops.

example.title: Agent-safe dependency addition
example.code: poetry add "fastapi@>=0.115,<1.0" poetry lock --check # Verify lock matches pyproject poetry install --sync # Ensure venv matches lock poetry run pytest -x # Quick sanity check git add pyproject.toml poetry.lock git commit -m "feat: add fastapi"
example.note: All mutations via poetry CLI; verification gates each step.

example.title: Agent hand-editing lock
example.code: # Agent tries to "fix" the lock by editing it directly Edit poetry.lock: change fastapi version from 0.115.0 to 0.116.0 # Breaks lock file; next poetry install fails
example.note: Lock is auto-generated; editing it by hand corrupts it.

section.name: cli-tools-and-commands
section.p: Poetry's CLI is the primary interface. Essential commands cover initialization, dependency management, lock file handling, and publishing.

rule.statement: Pin Poetry version in CI (e.g., pipx install poetry==2.0.1, not latest). Use poetry install --sync in CI to match lock exactly. Cache ~/.cache/pypoetry keyed on poetry.lock hash, not date.
rule.rationale: Unpinned Poetry can introduce breaking changes between minor versions (plugin churn, resolver changes). --sync cleans stale packages; caching on lock hash invalidates when dependencies change.

example.title: Essential Poetry commands
example.code: poetry new project poetry init # Existing dir poetry add fastapi uvicorn poetry add -G dev pytest mypy poetry update --dry-run # Preview before applying poetry update poetry lock # Explicit lock update poetry export -f requirements.txt --without-hashes # For Docker poetry build poetry publish
example.note: Covers bootstrap through publish; --dry-run before update; export for legacy CI.

template.purpose: Standard application with dev/test groups and tool configs.
Publishable library with metadata, classifiers, and optional docs group.
FastAPI project with async, SQLAlchemy, and testing stack.
Django project with DRF, Celery, and pytest-django.
Monorepo root with path dependencies to local packages.
GitHub Actions workflow with Poetry install and caching.
Multi-stage Dockerfile: Poetry build, then pip install in slim runtime.
